comments.text: Inspired by the recent advances, I + ChatGPT + Aristotle recently worked through this problem independently, and I wanted to leave a brief record of what came out of that exploration. We did not obtain any new number-theoretic bounds, nor resolve the conjecture. What we did was to isolate and formalize a precise conditional reduction that seems to be implicit in earlier work (notably Granville-Soundararajan and related discussions), but not written down as a standalone statement. Concretely, the obstruction to writing an odd integer $n$ as a squarefree number plus a power of two can be organized via the sets\[ A_p(n) := \{ k \in \mathbb{N} : 2^k \equiv n \pmod{p^2} \}, \]and one can show rigorously that, for odd $n$, each nonempty $A_p(n)$ is contained in a single residue class modulo $\operatorname{ord}_{p^2}(2)$, hence has upper asymptotic density at most $1/\operatorname{ord}_{p^2}(2)$. From this, one obtains the clean conditional implication that the conjecture holds provided\[ \sum_p \frac{1}{\operatorname{ord}_{p^2}(2)} < \infty. \]This does not advance the arithmetic input-in particular, it does not address Wieferich primes or provide new lower bounds on multiplicative orders-but it makes explicit that no further analytic or combinatorial difficulty remains beyond this summability condition. I am posting this simply to document the process and to clarify where the problem genuinely lies, rather than to claim novelty or priority. Very new to this field and this site, any word of advice would be greatly welcome.

Ok, I'm recording this as "did not find viable approach" in the wiki. I think everybody is new (regarding AI use at this level). I also think we're currently mostly ahead of others in using AI in math, so any experimentation welcome! (As long as it doesn't clutter - but Bloom will likely flag it himself in that case and then you'll know.)
Slowly getting the hang of it, thanks! Not my research area, but the problem seemed too interesting not to try and your writeup of #728 helped!
Nat, do you think a write-up for Arxiv documenting this is plausible here? Could connect over email.
Thanks! In general you can write up about anything you find interesting? Yes, feel free email me if you have any questions!

There are various links between the sum that you mention and this problem in the paper of Granville and Soundararajan. The implication you state is not one of them, however, and I do not believe you have a proof - certainly Granville and Soundararajan could not find one. For example, even assuming that the sum is $<1$, Granville and Soundararajan are only able to prove that almost all integers $n\leq x$ can be written as the sum of a squarefree number and a power of $2$. (Assuming just the convergence of this sum, they could prove that almost all odd integers are the sum of a power of $2$ and the product of a squarefree number and a bounded powerful number.) I assume that the implication you state was given to you by ChatGPT, which is wrongly paraphrasing the work of Granville and Soundararajan. I suggest that you do not write this up and submit it to the arXiv, since there does not seem to be anything new or correct here. It is great that you are taking an interest in this problem, and I encourage you to continue to think about it, but until you are at the stage where you are able yourself to understand and independently verify any 'proof' that an LLM provides, it is unlikely that you have made progress on this problem. Future comments which seem to be erroneous output from an LLM will not be shown here. Of course, if you believe I am being unreasonably sceptical, the best way to proceed is to formalise a proof of such an implication in Lean!

Given what Bloom said, you may want to share a draft here first. I can scrutinize whether there's some kind of misunderstanding. (Both have happened in the forum - that a result that looks good from AI turns out wrong, or turns out right.)

For soumya: I+ChatGPT mostly agrees with Bloom, in that there are links with G-S, but the conditional result seems unlikely to be easily obtainable. In particular, your comment that there is no further combinatorial difficulty seems unlikely given G-S's identification of covering system in Theorem 5 as a key combinatorial consideration. I've changed the wiki to "incorrect claim made". You're welcome to challenge this further e.g. if you believe it's correct and write up something I'll take a look. But don't be discouraged! I'd still encourage any comment that you think have value, provided some checks are performed and you're reasonably confident.
Thanks to both Nat and Professor Bloom for your invaluable feedback. I understand the shortcomings of the argument, will think about this further!

I forgot to mention It, but the paragraph relating this problem to Wieferich primes has a typo. As It is written, It suggests this problem implies there are arbitrarily many Wieferich primes, when in fact It implies that there are arbitrarily many Non-Wieferich primes. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
While the answer is most certainly affirmative, this is still mostly a computational problem, since we lack methods to decide such questions.
